record.title: Meriko Maida and Asako Tokuno | Interview 1 | November 2, 2013
record.subject: Japanese American Confinement Sites

point.title: Introductions / Stories about parents and grandparents

point.title: Experiences growing up in Richmond, California
point.keywords: children;cousins;diversity;elementary school;family;freedom;friends;Longfellow Junior High School;Richmond Union High School;Richmond, California;Stege Grammar School;student body
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Education, University of California;Japanese American Confinement Sites

point.title: Going to Japanese school for twelve years / Remembering working in her parent's nursery
point.keywords: business;carnations;de-bud;English language;flowers;grandparents;greenhouses;Japanese language;Japanese school;labor;nursery;parents;San Francisco, California;sisters;temporary laborers;transplanting;work
point.subjects: Commerce and Industry;Community and Identity;Education, University of California;Japanese American Confinement Sites;Natural Resources, Agriculture, and the Environment

point.keywords: Berkeley, California;California Flower Market;Chicago, Illinois;college;flowers;grades;Great Depression;Japanese language;Richmond, California;student;Subject A test;trains;truck;UC Berkeley;University of California, Berkeley
point.subjects: Commerce and Industry;Community and Identity;Education, University of California;Japanese American Confinement Sites;Natural Resources, Agriculture, and the Environment

point.title: Social life at UC Berkeley
point.keywords: boyfriend;dating;Half Moon Bay, California;Japanese American Men's Club;Japanese Americans;Jule's Donut Shop;relationships;Sather Gate YWCA;social life;socializing;Telegraph Avenue
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Education, University of California;Japanese American Confinement Sites

point.keywords: basketball;basketball team;big sister program;clubs;community;girl's basketball teams;J Leagues;Richmond, California;UC Berkeley
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Education, University of California;Japanese American Confinement Sites

point.title: Reactions to the bombing of Pearl Harbor / Remembering her mother destroying all of their Japanese items
point.keywords: alienation;bombing of Pearl Harbor;citizenship;fear;finals;Half Moon Bay, California;Japanese Americans;Japanese items;library;Montara, California;newspapers;Pearl Harbor;radios;shock;studying;University of California, Berkeley;War Civil Control Administration;WCCA

point.title: Making arrangements for the family nursery before being moved to the Tanforan Assembly Center
point.subjects: Community and Identity;Japanese American Confinement Sites

point.title: Experiences in the Tanforan Assembly Center / Remembering being uncertain about the future

point.title: Experiences with medical care in the internment camps / How interviewee's parents handled internment emotionally
point.subjects: Arts and Letters;Community and Identity;Japanese American Confinement Sites;Science, Technology, and Health

record.description:
Asako Tokuno and Meriko Maida grew up with their younger sister Junko Kimura, their parents, and grandparents running the family nursery before World War II in Richmond, California. They describe the demanding life of working a nursery in that period, and growing up as Japanese Americans in Richmond, California. They each discuss their family's summer travels and exploring California by fishing and camping, and their father's love of the outdoors.
Asako describes briefly attending UC Berkeley and then being forced immediately after Pearl Harbor to move to Half Moon Bay, then to Tanforan detention camp, and then to Topaz incarceration camp. She describes the structure of Topaz, working as a nurse at the hospital, and education.
Meriko Maida discusses her youth in Richmond, including her mother's cooking, transporting flowers, playing youth basketball with the Richmond Jinxes, Japanese school, Japanese movies, and her 1937 trip to Japan. She also talks about her time in the nursery, her activities at school in Richmond, and other aspects of her youth.
Unfortunately, tape three audio failed after the interview and could not be transcribed, thus we are missing the major discussion by Asako and Meriko of camp life. We had intended to re-record this portion of the interview, but sadly Asako fell ill and passed away before we could complete the interview.
In the final part of the interview, the sisters recall their experiences transferring out of camp when youngest sister Junko received a scholarship to the University of Rochester in New York. Asako and Meriko both worked while their sister Junko attended the university. Asako got engaged, moved to Minnesota and found work there while her husband was training in the Military Intelligence School. Asako recounts her travel to Japan after the war to be with her husband, who was part of the US military occupation, and then her return home with her first son, who was born in Japan.
record.transcript:
DUNHAM: It's November 2, 2013. This is David Dunham and Candice Fukumoto, for the Regional Oral History Office, here with Asako Tokuno, who has graciously invited us into her home. We're going to do an interview today about--well, her entire experience, but particularly focusing on her experiences at UC Berkeley and then what happened after the war and the executive order. But everything in between, as well. So I'll turn it over to Candice.
FUKUMOTO: We usually start at the beginning, with your full name, date and location of birth.
TOKUNO: Asako Maida Tokuno. I was born in September, on September 4, 1923, in Richmond, California, to my parents, Torayoshi Muraki Maida and my mother, Kane Maida. My dad was a Yoshi. I don't know if you recognize what that is. He took my mother's name, became part of the family, rather than she marrying him, to continue the family line. My mother's family did not have any sons. So that's why the name, it's my mother's name that my father took.
FUKUMOTO: What do you remember about your parents, and even your grandparents, immigrating to the United States?
TOKUNO: My grandfather was in Japan when some representative from--well, someone that was interested in recruiting help--went to Japan. This is from my understanding; my sister may have a different opinion about what happened. But from the best of my knowledge, they were recruiting workers to be in the nursery business. When my grandfather heard about it, he realized, at that point--it was in the mid-1800s--that there was no future for him in Japan, so he either signed up or came with the group. So he--what's the word--studied the nursery business with them. I think at first, though, he did some work in the grape fields, to earn some money and to have some of his own experiences on his own, when he first came. I'm not sure of the reason, but anyway, then eventually, he did go with the nursery people and learn how to raise flowers.
FUKUMOTO: Interesting. How did your parents meet?
TOKUNO: Actually, my grandfather and grandmother went to Japan, and they met my father and liked him. It's kind of a long story. My mother came with my grandmother. My mother came with grandmother, after my grandfather established himself. He worked with the nursery people and learned his trade for ten years, before he was able to move and buy his own land and build the greenhouses and the house, and then he called them over. This was in 1906, when the fire happened, the first time he sent the cable and said, "The house is ready. I want you to come." He had an adopted son, also at that time. So my grandmother and my mother and my uncle, adopted uncle, were told to come over--the earthquake happened, so he told my mother and grandmother to wait and for his son to come over. So he arrived first. I'm not sure; I think my mother was fourteen when she came with my grandmother. I'm not sure what year that was. But I think she was born in 1897; plus fourteen in 1921?
DUNHAM: Or '11.
TOKUNO: 1911. That's about a decade.
FUKUMOTO: So your family was linked to the nursery business, so that's why they came over to Richmond?
TOKUNO: Yes.
FUKUMOTO: To build the business.
TOKUNO: Yes.
FUKUMOTO: What is your memory of Richmond, growing up as a young girl in Richmond? What was life like?
TOKUNO: We had a great childhood. We lived not in the central city, of course; we were on the outskirts, where the greenhouses were. Had lots of freedom. We used to play out in the streets until dark. My cousins lived next door. My grandfather had set up his adopted son, and then my mother's place, two different properties. He lived in the main property, and then my uncle on an adjoining property. And then when my father came--well, they all lived together, except for my uncle. He had his own family. So we had cousins, grew up with cousins. We played a lot together. We roller skated, played hide and seek, all the games that kids do, and never had any worries that I can remember. Didn't really feel any discrimination, because I think Richmond was pretty much a town of many--. Not many, but a lot of Italians, lots of Portuguese. I can't remember, but there were several ethnicities, so that I didn't feel animosity, really, from anybody. Never had anybody say anything hurtful to me, for that matter.
FUKUMOTO: Did you mingle a lot with the other ethnic groups?
TOKUNO: We all played with everyone. Yeah, I do. In fact, I'm still in touch with, until recently, three of my classmates from kindergarten. We talk to each other regularly. Now there're only two. One in Red Bluff and one in San Leandro. No, Hayward; she moved to Hayward. So I'm in touch with them.
FUKUMOTO: Well, that's wonderful!
TOKUNO: They're ninety-two and I'm ninety, but we were kindergarteners at the same time. Went all the way through high school together.
FUKUMOTO: That's really special.
TOKUNO: It is, yeah. We talk about that a lot. But we played mostly with my cousins and the boys that lived close by. Japanese nursery people, also.
FUKUMOTO: And the school that you attended? What was your elementary school called?
TOKUNO: Elementary was Stege Elementary. I think they call it Stege Grammar School. And then Longfellow Junior High. That was sixth to ninth. Was it ninth? Sixth to ninth? Yes. Tenth through twelfth was Richmond Union High School, I'm pretty sure. Yeah, Richmond Union High was--that doesn't sound right; two years, only. Well, nine to twelve? I should ask my sister.
FUKUMOTO: It has changed over the years. Junior high now--
TOKUNO: Now it's middle school.
FUKUMOTO: Middle school is sixth through eighth.
TOKUNO: Yes. But back then, we had a junior high, and it was seventh, eighth, and ninth.
FUKUMOTO: Were you active in school? What kind of things were you involved in?
TOKUNO: Grammar school?
FUKUMOTO: Grammar, middle.
TOKUNO: Middle school, I was a commissioner, at one point. We used to monitor the halls, the hallways, during recess, and make sure people coming up the stairs or going down, the boys weren't misbehaving. We stood at the top of the stairwell. There was a pillar, and we'd stand and keep an eye on them. We'd issue tickets if they were naughty. Then we'd have court; we'd have a regular trial. So we would go. If we did pass out a ticket, we'd have to attend and let them know what happened, and they would always have some price to pay, a punishment of some kind. That was kind of fun, because it was like having a real justice system in school. That kept the kids in good, I think, being good.
Ran for student body--I was secretary a lot. Secretary of the eighth grade. Ninth grade, I think I ran for something, but I did not win. I think it was president, or student body president. Aimed too high. I can still remember my speech. It was close to Christmas time. So it was kind of funny.
DUNHAM: What do you remember about the speech?
TOKUNO: It was asking Santa Claus for help to become president of the student body. Something silly. But then high school, let's see. I was not as active. There was a lot of homework, I remember. But I can't recall that I was very involved in student activities, or even athletics. I loved gym, PE, but--.
DUNHAM: How often were you going to the Japanese school? Was that all through--?
TOKUNO: On Saturdays and Sundays. Saturday, from 9:00 to--hmm. It was a long day. I think it was 9:00 to 2:00. We took our lunch, so it must've been past lunch. And then Sundays, from 8:00 to 12:00.
DUNHAM: All the way through high school?
TOKUNO: I went all the way through. I did twelve years of Japanese school.
FUKUMOTO: Wow. Did you speak English and Japanese in your own home, or what was the primary language?
TOKUNO: Yeah, we spoke Japanese to my parents. As much as we understood and could speak it, but pretty well, I think. Because we went to Japanese school, most likely. Then English with my siblings. I had an older sister and a younger sister. Poor middle child.
FUKUMOTO: Middle child. Were you pretty close to your sisters?
TOKUNO: I think so. We're still very close, actually. My older sister lives with me. She's a hard act to follow. She's my role model.
DUNHAM: So your older sister, you said, is four years older?
TOKUNO: Yes. She's ninety-four. Because she was born in February, and I was born in September, so that stretches it out. But yeah, 1920 and 1923, so three years, if you, say, actually add the years.
DUNHAM: And your younger sister?
TOKUNO: In 1926, so she's three years younger.
DUNHAM: Is she still with us?
TOKUNO: Yes. She lives in Richmond.
FUKUMOTO: She's still in Richmond.
TOKUNO: Mm-hm.
FUKUMOTO: What was in your family's nursery?
TOKUNO: It was carnations. In the beginning my dad grew roses. But not for such a long time. I can't remember the reason. I don't remember why he stopped, but suddenly we were raising carnations, and that was what he stayed with, carnations.
FUKUMOTO: Did you work in the nursery?
TOKUNO: A lot. We look back, and it was really fun, but it was hard work at the time. We complained a lot. But especially in the summer. That was when the new plants were transplanted, so there was a lot of work for us. A lot of work for women, because of all the buds--. To bring the flower to its fullest capacity, we would have to de-bud. Because they would grow, and they would have buds. So we'd have this canvas bag around our necks, with an opening, and we would de-bud, drop them in. Then in the transplanting, carnations grow, and you have to support them. So we would have these laths, kind of, that set up a frame; and then wires, five rows of wires that would be drawn taut across. Then we would put string across. One person would be on the other side of the bench to connect the string. They're growing in a bench. You've seen them in the pictures in the Blossoms and Thorns. There may even be some of those left, where you can see how they were supported. Then as they grew, then the second layer, they would add wires and we would put more strings across. A lot of women's work. And then bunching them, also. We used to pick them--my mom used to pick them--and then we would bunch.
FUKUMOTO: Wow. Do you remember how big your nursery was?
TOKUNO: Yeah. We had five hothouses. Now, the measurements, I don't remember, but five and then two. The boiler house, what they called, where they made the steam to heat the flowers in the winter, was in between; and two; and then five. And then we had a huge--we called it the new house. It was built--I'm not sure how many years between the two. I think my grandfather set up the first--eight, was it? What'd I say? Six, two, eight. And then my dad built this huge greenhouse. Anyway, very long, but the extent of the six that were on the side of the road. She could draw a picture, but that's--can you visualize?
FUKUMOTO: Yeah, I was just trying to imagine even doing that work.
TOKUNO: I know!
FUKUMOTO: For a long time, all day, that may hours, sounds really hard.
TOKUNO: Yes. Well, we had laborers, kind of, that helped. Usually two or--young fellows that would look for a part-time job or whatever.
DUNHAM: Were those usually Japanese?
TOKUNO: The permanent ones were Japanese. We had older ones that would live--we had a workman's house, we called it, where they stayed.
DUNHAM: Temporary laborers, it varied what their ethnicity was?
TOKUNO: Temporary ones would--let's see. I don't know if they stayed. I can't remember whether they came--I think they drove to work. But actually, some of them did say, but don't ask me where. They might've shared the lodging.
DUNHAM: What was a typical day for your mom like, when you were growing up then?
TOKUNO: A lot of work. She'd be up and then be out. We had hothouses, and usually she would be the one cutting. She would cut the flowers and then carry as many as she could, and then bring them to the flower house, where we bunched--we would put them in barrels of water. They would be sitting in the barrels for some time. Then when she was through, then she commenced to bunch them into twenty-four in a bunch, at a time. Then wrap them. My dad used to deliver them to the flower market, wholesale flower market in San Francisco.
Then later--we're getting ahead, but after the war he had someone that came to pick up and deliver and take care of that. But my grandfather used to put his flowers in a Japanese woven basket--kind of like that, about that size--and lay them in very carefully; and then tie a rope around it, carry them on his back to the bus. From the bus to the train to the ferry, and take them over to the market. How he got from the ferry to the--well, there were streetcars in San Francisco. It would be not even the crack of dawn; it would be very early in the morning. My sister says that my grandmother used to light the way, because it was so dark. She'd take the flashlight and go with him. Then she would go back home when he would go. I just can't imagine how he did that for--he was younger, of course. But yeah, that's what they did.
DUNHAM: Was the business affected much by the Great Depression in the thirties?
TOKUNO: In the thirties, yes. It made a difference. The flower market board of directors or whoever was in charge decided, to keep the whole nursery business going, to do something. They sent some of the--. Whoever was willing to go went to Los Angeles, went to Seattle. My dad went to Chicago. They thought that they could sell the flowers back east, by sending them by train. Back then, they didn't have refrigerated cars, so the flowers were weakened by the time they got to this shop that he was renting. So he didn't have any flowers to sell, except what was locally available. So I don't know how long he stayed, but I think it was quite some time before he came back. He drove a truck. I can see the truck he had. It said "California Flower Market." It was a panel truck. He drove that back. He must've driven it there and driven it back. It had the Chicago address on it. I wonder if he bought it in Chicago. I have to check that with my sister.
DUNHAM: Did he say anything about his experiences in Chicago, what Chicago was like at the time?
TOKUNO: There was a lot of snow; it was cold. Let's see what else he said about that. No, I can't remember. I'm sorry.
DUNHAM: No, not at all.
FUKUMOTO: Hard times, though. So how did you end up at UC Berkeley? What was that path? What kind of pushed you to go to UC Berkeley?
TOKUNO: Well, I think my folks expected me to. I would always bring home good grades. They just had that expectation, so I followed through with what they assumed. Plus, I must've had some desire to go. Took the SAT--well, they didn't have SAT, but we had what you call Subject A back then. Everyone didn't take Subject A, but I think those who were college bound did. My sister, on the one hand--maybe because she didn't really want to go to college, my dad sent her to Japan. But she chose that rather than go to college. So I think maybe I felt like I should go to college. So I took the Subject A, managed to get through it and pass it.
FUKUMOTO: What did you study? What was your focus at Berkeley?
TOKUNO: I have to tell you, I was such a romanticist or lived in a fantasy world. I used to think it would be so neat to be an airline stewardess. That's what I put on my form in my resume. And of course, we had a counselor that talked with us. When I had my turn to go over my courses with her and all the requirements that I would need to fulfill to do whatever it was that I said I'd like to do, she said, "I really think with your background, you should take Oriental languages." I thought about it, I thought, why not? I took twelve years of Japanese school. So I thought, well, that made sense. It was much more realistic, anyway. So that's my major.
FUKUMOTO: So Japanese was your major? So you took literature? What kind of courses did you take?
TOKUNO: Oh, gosh, there're about three Oriental classes. They called it Oriental languages; that was the designation for those courses. It involved Chinese; there was one Chinese class. And I remember, Dr. Booberg was his name, the professor. Then the Japanese, I remember mostly--I think one was conversational, and one was more grammar. Might've had some history.
FUKUMOTO: Did you enjoy those classes?
TOKUNO: Yes. We had a wonderful teacher. She was a missionary in Japan, and she spoke like a native. Yeah, she was great.
FUKUMOTO: Do you remember where you lived?
TOKUNO: I lived in the same place I was born, born and raised, Richmond.
FUKUMOTO: So you commuted?
TOKUNO: Yeah, I took the bus. Took the bus from San Pablo Avenue to University Avenue. That was before streetcars. Transferred to a streetcar on University to the campus.
FUKUMOTO: What was social life like?
TOKUNO: Now, which one?
FUKUMOTO: At Berkeley, when you were going to school?
TOKUNO: Oh. That was what it was, a lot of social life, as I remember. It seemed like. Let's see. Well, you start right off, I think it's the Chancellor's reception or something like that, or President's reception. I can't remember which one. Or maybe both of them had a reception. Anyway, so I thought that was something you had to do. I attended that. I don't remember too much about it. Then they had a Japanese-American men's club, which would have some socials. And I remember a Christmas party that we had--it was the only year I was there for Christmas with, I think it was the freshman class of Japanese Americans, small group.
DUNHAM: About how many of you were there, do you think?
TOKUNO: It was one table, I would say about fifteen to twenty, maybe. Twenty at the most. Yeah, it was a long table, with probably ten on either side.
DUNHAM: Did you have activities with I House [International House] or with the Japanese Women's Student Clubhouse on Hearst?
TOKUNO: I didn't, but I joined the Sather Gate Y and met a lot of nice people there. I spent a lot of time there.
DUNHAM: What did you do there?
TOKUNO: Oh. What did we do? Oh, my gosh. I don't remember what we did.
DUNHAM: Were there dances?
TOKUNO: The only dance I remember with the Japanese Americans was the men's club had a dance in their clubhouse. But I do remember going to Jule's. I don't know if Jule's is there anymore, a donut shop.
DUNHAM: Where was that?
TOKUNO: Fellows would ask me to go have coffee. "Why don't we go have coffee?" I remember having a lot of donuts and coffee.
FUKUMOTO: [laughs] I like donuts and coffee.
TOKUNO: I did. Those Jule's donuts were really good. And they were like a nickel, a cup of coffee, and the donuts probably were five or ten cents.
DUNHAM: Where was that?
TOKUNO: Jule's, right on Telegraph Avenue.
DUNHAM: On Telegraph. Yeah, I don't think it's there.
FUKUMOTO: It's not there.
TOKUNO: Oh, it doesn't look the same at all. Telegraph looks so different. Everything is just so squeezed now and so busy, because it's so--I don't know, they've just put up so many buildings.
DUNHAM: Well, what was the dating life like there?
TOKUNO: Well, it was pretty exciting, because I didn't go to a lot of them, but I was asked to a lot of them. But there was only so much I could do. I had to commute, for one, and I don't remember any fellow saying, "I'll pick you up." They didn't have cars back then. So it was a matter of making my way to wherever.
FUKUMOTO: Did you date mostly Japanese men, or all?
TOKUNO: I had a boyfriend already. Not already; I shouldn't say that. As I say, we had part-time workers that helped my dad. We had three young guys that were coming on a regular basis. They lived in the workmen's house in the back. Actually, they were orphans. Mr. Takahashi lived in Montara, which was near Half Moon Bay. He had a huge set up. He had a home and then dormitories, like. And he would befriend these orphans. I don't know if they we're from Japan. They must've been from here. Or abandoned or whatever. It happened back then, too. I guess he'd been among the Japanese Americans, and these boys, he'd raise them. So my dad knew Mr. Takahashi, because he raised flowers, saw him at the nursery. And of course, he was very helpful, when it came to providing some workers.
So these boys, being from out of town, Half Moon Bay, had acquaintances that they knew from living near the people that lived in Half Moon Bay. And one of them was this fellow who would come over to visit with them. So then we became acquainted, sometimes, with some of them. One of them especially was very personable, and was very interested. So then we, I guess you'd call, went together. Anyway, so I was not available, I suppose I could say, maybe.
DUNHAM: That started during high school?
TOKUNO: It was, yeah, the last year--I was in high school still, yeah.
FUKUMOTO: Do you have any other questions in her Cal Berkeley experience?
TOKUNO: This is my confessions. [they laugh] A naughty girl.
DUNHAM: No, no.
TOKUNO: I'll have a sip of water.
DUNHAM: Okay, please, please.
TOKUNO: Can I get you anything? I have tea ready to go.
DUNHAM: Oh, I think we're fine. Well, was there anything else in the transition from high school in Richmond to UC Berkeley, and classes and campus life, that stands out for you? Was it a pretty seamless transition? Or was there anything that surprised you?
TOKUNO: Oh, it was very social. We were very protected in Richmond. It was just the circle of the Japanese school kids, students, and the neighbors and all the nursery people. So our social life--we had a basketball team, and the coach was kind of a new person and really intrigued us all. Not intrigued us, but he was intriguing because he was somebody we didn't know, an outsider. That brought some excitement to our lives.
DUNHAM: Non-Japanese?
TOKUNO: No, no, he was Japanese, a Nisei.
DUNHAM: But he was outside that little community?
TOKUNO: Yes, he was someone new.
DUNHAM: Did you play basketball?
TOKUNO: Yes, we had a team. We had a girls club, actually. A girls club, Richmond Jinxes.
DUNHAM: Who did you play against? Did you have other teams?
TOKUNO: Yeah, the Bay Area teams, different teams that we would go play. The men had a team, and they would play the men's team; and we would play the girls team. So we would have to have rides, and we would go with the fellows. That provided our transportation and where to go. Yeah, that was for a short time. It was fun.
DUNHAM: Did you have a gym that you played in inside?
TOKUNO: Seems to me we played at the high school gym. I don't know where that was.
DUNHAM: But was it the early days of the J Leagues then? Was it called that then?
TOKUNO: I think so.
DUNHAM: Candice is a basketball coach.
TOKUNO: Oh. Yeah, we used to have fun. I guess it was tournament, but we never won anything. But it was fun to travel around. We played Stockton and Alameda and Berkeley. I can't remember where else. Stockton was as far as we went. I don't remember Sacramento, if they had a team or not; but I remember Stockton. I guess because they were really good. But that was the girls--and our girls club used to have fundraisings. We'd sell donuts. And have dances and we'd invite the fellows. They would sit in the corner and the girls would sit in the corner. That was about the extent of the part. But we tried real hard.
FUKUMOTO: Was it 1940 that you entered at Cal?
TOKUNO: I graduated high school June of '41. September of '41, I started Cal. We had big sister program at Cal. Or they had a big sister program. On probably the first day, I did go with her, my big sister, who was one of the nursery girls. She was a senior, I think. But I knew her, and of course, she was at our Japanese school. So I knew her well and felt comfortable, but I don't remember really having her lead me by the hand. She got me onto campus, and that was it from there. But yeah, they did assign big sisters. Do they still have a program like that?
FUKUMOTO: They probably do. I would be surprised if they didn't have something like that.
TOKUNO: Yeah, you need help, in a way, to learn the ropes in the beginning, where the buildings are. You don't know where to go.
FUKUMOTO: It's so big.
TOKUNO: Totally lost.
FUKUMOTO: Do you remember hearing of Pearl Harbor? At that point in your life, when you heard about the bombing of Pearl Harbor, do you remember where you were, what you were doing?
TOKUNO: Oh, yes! Do I ever! [coughs]
DUNHAM: Do you want some water? Or do you need to take a break?
TOKUNO: That's what happens every now and then. Yes. We were studying. This was December 7 of 1941. At that time, we had finals after the holidays, as I remember. So I was going to go up to the library to study--presumably to study. But I'd heard the news, but I still determined to go out. Got my books and I went to the library. I walked into the library, and every student there--there were a lot of Japanese Americans; they always studied in the library. What was the name of that library? It was a big one.
DUNHAM: Doe?
TOKUNO: No. Everybody had the newspaper. They were reading the paper. I don't remember anyone having a radio, but it's possible someone had a radio. They had radios, those days, right? Portables, '41, after all. So we really couldn't study. It was just such dreadful news. Of course, no one knew what was going to happen. We probably all thought the worst, but never dreamed the kind of thing that did happen. I think we were more concerned about our safety. We didn't think we'd be put into camps or something like that. So yeah, it was very dramatic.
FUKUMOTO: Your parents, what was their reaction?
TOKUNO: Immediate reaction?
FUKUMOTO: Yeah, it could be immediate or just even--
TOKUNO: I think they were shocked, as well. But let's see. I think they were frightened, more than anything else, because they really were not citizens. They were from Japan. They were what you call enemy aliens, even though they were here to stay and their kids were citizens. I think they felt very alienated and intimidated. More scared, I'm sure, as to what was going to happen to them, because they were at the country's mercy, government's mercy, being they were not citizens. So I guess we were all in a state of shock, too, not expecting something like that.
FUKUMOTO: What is your memory of just even leading up to the executive order? What did your family do? I think we've read that your parents were taken earlier than you, right?
TOKUNO: Yes.
FUKUMOTO: How did you guys deal with that?
TOKUNO: They had to go right away, because they were--Richmond was called a vital zone. What was what they called Richmond?
DUNHAM: Zone A?
TOKUNO: Well, Zone A, but they called it vital--
DUNHAM: Because it was waterfront?
TOKUNO: Defense. Some kind of, yeah, very primary area of--because of the shipyards, the oil. The oil tanks and Standard Oil. So I suppose the government was worried about spies, or anything, traitors, that might happen. So that was the first thing. They had to leave right away. They had hardly any notice. They almost packed in the night and had to go, zip, off to--they did go to Mr. Takahashi, who had the boys, the orphans that he--I can't think of words. So that's where my folks went, to Montara, which was close to Half Moon Bay. I guess it's really Half Moon Bay, that area. We were there, not knowing still. They didn't issue the executive order until February. So we stayed, and then gradually, we realized that probably we would have to go, too. But our group, that area--at least for us--we were permitted to stay. We didn't have to leave until--I don't know whether we had choice or not in the matter.
But before this happened, now, before this, after my folks went, we had to gather things. My mother had already destroyed the Buddhist altar and she had broken sashimi knives and buried things and burned things that were not--just the fact that they were Japanese. But very precious to her, but she was crying as she did it. Those kind of things, that she was able to do before she left. Then WCCA, which was the War Control something or other [Wartime Civil Control Administration]--.
DUNHAM: Is it WRCA?
TOKUNO: WCCA. Wartime Citizens Control? I'm sorry. It might be somewhere in the computer.
DUNHAM: We can look that up.
TOKUNO: WCCA confiscated things. We had to take our radios to the police station, cameras. I can't remember what else. My sister did all that because I was busy studying.
DUNHAM: Is this your older sister, or was your older sister in Japan?
TOKUNO: No, no, she's home. She's back. So she took care of those things. Then where did I leave off just now? When I went back and tell you about this?
FUKUMOTO: Well, no, you were just--kind of as your parents are getting ready to leave and having to destroy things that represent anything Japanese, and you and your sister having to take care of some of that stuff.
TOKUNO: Had to take care of those things. Then we stayed on, because all of the holidays, beginning with February. February was not that critical, but March--I think it was Easter--and then--no, February is Valentine's Day. All the good days for selling the carnations followed, until May, Mother's Day, Easter, I can't remember, depending on when Easter fell. But all the big holidays, where the carnations were really in big demand. So we stayed, and we took care of the nurseries, and we had help from these boys that were there, that we had working for us. We managed to get all that done. I remember even dying the flowers for St. Patrick's Day. We always dyed them green. So we had to then be taken to Tanforan. We were bused to Tanforan. That was the first gathering. Assembly center. It's called the assembly center. Then from there, we went to Topaz. So that was from May to September in Tanforan, while they were building the barracks in Topaz.
DUNHAM: Now, back to your parents. The reason you didn't have to leave, was it because you were citizens, because you were Nisei? That was the distinction, okay.
TOKUNO: They let us stay. I don't know if we got special permission, but we were there and stayed until--I don't think they had orders for us to go. We just stayed until the orders came. Luckily, I think it went up to May, or pretty much May.
DUNHAM: So what do you remember when you were getting ready to go to Tanforan, and about Tanforan?
TOKUNO: Well, before that, we went to stay with my folks. We were able to leave the property. My father came back a number of times to make arrangements for the nursery. Somebody to keep an eye on it and take care of the flowers. It was all word of mouth, and it was with our neighbor, who was an Italian fellow, who had taken over the Japanese family, the Fukushimas, that was our closest neighbor. So they had talked to this fellow. What was his name?
DUNHAM: I've heard his name, and now I'm drawing a blank, too. Sorry.
TOKUNO: Well, he was an Italian fellow. He was very nice. My dad talked to him; he said yes, he'll take good care of it. Of course, there had to be more important arrangements than that, but he managed to take care of them, my father. Then that's when we joined him in Half Moon Bay, with the Takahashis.
FUKUMOTO: So you were working at the nursery and still going to school up to May, is that right?
TOKUNO: I have my leave of absence, dated February 19. I still have it. February 19, 1942. That's really close to the day of the executive order, I went over to get the leave of absence.
FUKUMOTO: That must've been really stressful. How did you handle all that pressure? Did you just, you've got to do this, not think about it? Or did you and your sister get a chance to ever just talk about what was happening? Or did you guys not talk about it?
TOKUNO: I don't remember talking; we probably did. We probably wondered what would happen, what's happening to our parents. But I can't remember if we were--I don't think there was any phone communication. I don't remember talking to them during that time. But we stayed several--I'm trying to think now. If we left the nursery after the--must've been Mother's Day. May would be the last time. The dates escape me, as far as when we went to Tanforan. It might've been June, because we spent time with Mr. Takahashi and his wife. We had shots. We had to get our flu shots during that time. All kinds of inoculations, for protection. The time just escapes me, how much time that was when we spent there, before we had to go to the camps.
DUNHAM: Did you know of any Japanese Americans who moved East, voluntarily relocated?
TOKUNO: Yes. One of the nursery families that I know for sure was the Kawais. They had relatives in Idaho, and I believe that's where they went. The others, I don't remember. I think they were all in Topaz.
DUNHAM: So what was your experience like in Tanforan? Do you remember how long you were there and what your accommodations there were?
TOKUNO: Well, I remember the accommodations. Some of the people, of course, were in the horse stalls. But we were very fortunate. I'm not sure how fortunate, but they had put up so quickly, these barrack-type buildings. Two apartments on the end were small, for a family of four, two adults and two children. Then probably four, in the center, three or four different bigger apartments just open, with this much crack between the floorboards. Just clear all the way through. Just open.
FUKUMOTO: They were just open. So no curtains or no--.
TOKUNO: Well, no curtains, but I mean the whole barracks, the top of it was just, from that end to the other, it was just open. No partitions, that's the word I'm trying to think of. So my mom--and Dad must've helped--put up ropes and sheets to section off, for some privacy. So the sheets made rooms. So it was quite a large room--that was a good thing--as opposed to the ones in Topaz, which were very small.
DUNHAM: What was the food and other--?
TOKUNO: Oh, very bad. It improved as time went on. I think about a week, everyone was sick. Yeah. The food was spoiled or--it was not good. We took our place and waited in these long lines, where the people sit in the bleachers.
FUKUMOTO: The dining hall?
TOKUNO: No, it wasn't a dining hall, but the grandstand, for the racing. They had it set up so that we would get the food and go take it back to our barracks. Yeah, it was very bad. But you can imagine, this was a very temporary setup, made to do and take care of everybody, numbers like that.
FUKUMOTO: Did you know that you were going to be sent to Utah?
TOKUNO: No, we didn't know where we were going to go.
FUKUMOTO: So on that day when they just started to send families, they just called your name? How did that work? How did that happen, as far as sending you to Utah?
TOKUNO: That's all so vague. That part, I don't remember. We were driven there. I don't know how. I know it was not a bus. I just don't recall how. We must've had some kind of transportation, because we certainly didn't have a car. I imagine it had to be a bus, for that area of Montara. As you remember, my boyfriend, Sam, was in that town of Half Moon Bay; that's where they lived, very close to Montara. So they were our neighbors, turned out to be our neighbors, because we must've been taken, one group, in the transportation they provided to take us to the camp, to Tanforan. So I assume that that's why we were in the same barracks with our neighbors.
FUKUMOTO: Then from Tanforan to Topaz, the same thing? Probably a car or a bus that you rode in?
TOKUNO: We were loaded onto trains. I don't remember how we got to the trains, but we were put into trains. Very old trains. The cold, shades all down, armed guards. That was pretty scary.
DUNHAM: Was that especially on the trains, or was that all the time at Tanforan?
TOKUNO: There were always guards at Tanforan. I don't remember seeing them; they were on the perimeters. People would come to visit, and they would be outside looking in and bring things, pass it through--I think it was like a cyclone fence. If they had food or whatever to give to their friends, that's the way they did it. But it was not like open camp, you walk in, "Here, I want this for so-and-so." I guess they communicated. I think we were able to write letters and cards and things like that. I got a card from my friend from Cal, one of my friends, telling me what the conditions were like, telling me what I should bring, what I should be prepared for and that kind of thing, which was really nice.
DUNHAM: A friend who was already at the camp, at Topaz--
TOKUNO: That was already at the camp.
DUNHAM: --or a different camp?
TOKUNO: At the Tanforan Camp. At that point, no one was in the Topaz camp yet; it was still being built.
FUKUMOTO: But your whole family was together when you went to Topaz.
TOKUNO: Yes. Thank goodness, yes. My grandfather was still with us. He was eighty-two, I believe. Because when we came back, he was eighty-five and lived for three more years. Eighty-eight. Yeah.
DUNHAM: Your grandmother had already passed?
TOKUNO: Yes, she had passed. She had a cerebral hemorrhage in '72, I think it was.
FUKUMOTO: And you were saying that in Topaz, the housing was a lot smaller than--
TOKUNO: Yes. The barracks. They were smaller. Maybe the space seemed larger in the Tanforan because of all the openness, but yeah, it was a smaller room. It was a disappointment when we got there, because there was just nothing in there. I think there was one potbellied stove at one end and coiled-spring beds that were stacked; we had to take them down. That was all there was. When we got there, they gave us a mattress cover, some kind of a rough canvas-type thing. They told us that we should go and get some straw, to make our own mattresses. They indicated where we should go to get it, and everybody made a trail and went to get the straw. We all filled our mattresses with the straw. They were cot-size, so they would fit right on the cot. They were not very comfortable.
FUKUMOTO: I would imagine food in Topaz was not so tasty either, right? Or did it get better?
TOKUNO: I think they tried really hard. The employees were all inmates, so to speak. So they were Japanese cooks. Probably Issei, Nisei. They were experienced, because they volunteered to work in the mess halls. And the waitresses were volunteers. Everybody got paid a stipend. The cooks probably got $19, which was considered the upper wage, I guess professional wage. Then the waitresses, everybody else got $18, I think. $19 was the top; $16 was the lowest. That was for the small things we could buy. They did have a PX, eventually. Not in the beginning, but eventually they set up a PX. That was run by the inmates. What's the other word for--? Evacuees? Maybe that's a better word.
FUKUMOTO: It depends; it almost is the same.
DUNHAM: It's heavily debated, right? And you got a job pretty early on, as well? Is that right? Or when did you get a job?
TOKUNO: I think everybody wanted to do something. What were you going to do? So let's see, in the very beginning I don't know if I went to work right away. I enjoyed my leisure, I suppose. But then we would get informational fliers or pamphlets, and gradually, they did start a newspaper, the Topaz Times. In the beginning, it would be like fliers and they would say, classes will begin for such-and-such, and something is being set up at rec hall such-and-such. You know the layout at the camp?
FUKUMOTO: I'm not very familiar with Topaz.
TOKUNO: There was one dining room, dining hall, right in the center. Then another huge building that was a laundry and shower and toilet facilities, all in this one building, all open. Then there was a rec hall, which was beside the barracks there, lined up. So you couldn't tell the rec hall from the barracks, but there was always one rec hall at the end, and then the barracks were lined up. So these fliers would let us know what was going on at such-and-such, rec hall number--. Thirty-nine blocks, I think there were, in the whole Topaz camp, to the best of my knowledge. There might've been more, but thirty-nine.
I read in one of them that they were going to have courses for nurse's aides. They needed nurses. I thought, "Boy, that sounds great. I'll go take the course and work at the hospital." It was, I think, a two-week course by a trained nurse, Mrs. Takahashi. Two weeks, we had. We were taught the basics and materia medica. We learned how to give inoculations later. But we essentially did about what nurses do, because there were not enough nurses. So we were given a lot of leeway to give inoculations, shots and medications, which is not what nurses aides did. So we were a grade above nurse's aides, but not quite nurses. So I did that. Worked really different shifts. They had the day shift, night shift, morning shift, and graveyard shift. Worked in the men's ward and pediatrics ward. Well, anyway, you transfer around.
Then at one point, I had to be a patient at the hospital; I had to have my appendix taken out. So after that, they were more careful, back then. They didn't want you lifting things, like bodies; we'd give sponge baths for the patients. So I had to find another job, and that's when I went to personnel. I applied for a job at the personnel office, which was the main office for the administration. Took a typing test and made it that way.
FUKUMOTO: Do you remember, though, the hospital part? Was it pretty busy? Did a lot of people get sick, just because of the weather? I would imagine that would be tough.
TOKUNO: Oh, yeah. Yeah.
FUKUMOTO: We're not prepared for the weather, right? Especially coming from--
TOKUNO: Yeah. In fact, in the very beginning, it was very--they didn't really have a hospital per se; it was kind of like one of the barracks, I think, that they used. You could hear people--like ladies in childbirth--screaming, from outside. But once the hospital was built, the doctor staff was regular doctors. They were young. They'd probably just finished their--in fact, the doctor who operated on me, I was his first surgery.
DUNHAM: Did you know that going in?
TOKUNO: Yes. He was out of Cal. In fact, he was my doctor after we got back here. He set up an office in Oakland. I think my second child--was he the doctor for the second one? Anyway. Yeah, he was a good doctor. Thank goodness.
DUNHAM: And was he Japanese American?
TOKUNO: Oh, yes.
DUNHAM: Were all of the medical staff?
TOKUNO: Yes. Yes. The administrator of it was Caucasian. Most of the administrators were all Caucasian. The engineer that ran the place and who planned, and the financial man was Mr. Bell. I knew this because of being in the personnel office. I used to do their payroll, so I knew the names pretty well. They were all the top men.
FUKUMOTO: Your father and your grandfather and your mother, how did they deal with all of this during this time? Did they keep a lot of things in, to just show, "we're going to be okay"? Or have you seen them break down, even?
TOKUNO: Never.
FUKUMOTO: Never broke down.
TOKUNO: Never break down. Yeah. They were strong. They accepted what was going on. In fact, I heard several of them--or several people down the road, later--saying that that was the first time they had had a vacation. Some of these people--especially in Sacramento or places where they had raised strawberries, which was hard work, or picking fruits and harvesting and that kind of work--they worked every day, every day, had no relief. So it was like a vacation to them, to be in the camps. As long as they were protected and fed. And I think they got wages, too; mess hall cooks and--. My mother just loved it. She went to classes; she took calligraphy and poetry. She wrote Japanese poetry. Because they set up classes for everybody. It was really well done.
FUKUMOTO: It was all organized by Japanese, for Japanese?
TOKUNO: Really nice. Yes.
FUKUMOTO: So that it was basically the community just, "we have to do something"?
TOKUNO: And we had the cream of the crop of the teachers there. Like Newton Tani--I don't know if you've heard of him--he was a violin teacher. Taught my sister, who had been taking violin many years. In fact, she got a scholarship to the Eastman School of Music at the University of Rochester, from camp.
DUNHAM: That was your younger--?
TOKUNO: My younger sister. She's still playing the violin. She plays for shut-in patients, shut-ins. So it's wonderful that she kept it up. Same with the artists that were there. Chiura Obata. You know him. He's a very well known professor at Cal. His son taught anatomy. There was a lot of talent that was available to us.
DUNHAM: We're going to take a break. We're here at the end of the tape.
TOKUNO: Oh, you're near the end? That was an hour?
DUNHAM: Yeah. Yeah, it was.
TOKUNO: Oh, I practically talked a full hour.
[End of Session]
